title: Cooking Oil on Linen
timeEstimate: 15–45 minutes; dry-side oil work first, wetclean only if safe
stainChemistry: Cooking oil on linen is a lipid/triglyceride stain on absorbent cellulose. Linen wicks oil quickly through the open weave, and heat can oxidize/polymerize residual oil into a yellow shadow. Professional handling should start dry-side so oil is lifted before water spreads it; wetclean/launder only after construction, dye stability, and care label are cleared.
whyThisWorks: Dry-side solvent work dissolves and moves the oil without driving it wider with water. Absorbent powder/blotter controls redeposition. Neutral synthetic detergent/wet-side cleanup removes residual surfactant-soluble oil only after dry-side removal. Linen-specific finishing is the risk: cool process, low mechanical action, reshape/block, and no heat until fully clear.
defaultAssumption: Simple washable flat linen can be wetcleaned after dry-side oil removal. Structured, lined, trimmed, vintage, dark-dyed, or dry-clean-only linen stays professional-only and should not receive a home-style Dawn/toothbrush protocol.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Good light, towel, magnification if needed
spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm washable vs dry-clean-only, dye stability, lining, trim, sizing, and age. Structured, lined, trimmed, vintage, or unstable dye = dry-side only and professional finishing. Do not wet-brush linen before this check.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dry-Side Oil Spotter / Volatile Dry Solvent
spottingProtocol.equipment: Spotting board or absorbent towel, bone/spatula, blotter
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place absorbent under the stain. Apply dry-side oil spotter or volatile dry solvent sparingly from the outside edge toward center. Tamp/feather gently; blot and move to clean absorbent often. Goal is oil transfer, not saturation.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Fuller’s earth or clean absorbent powder; soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: After solvent work, cover with absorbent powder or blotter to pull residual oil/solvent. Brush away gently. Repeat dry-side pass only if an oily halo remains.

spottingProtocol.technique: Bounded wet-side flush after dry-side removal
spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD, cool water, steam/air gun only if appropriate
spottingProtocol.instruction: If the garment is safe for water, use neutral synthetic detergent with cool water to remove residual emulsifiable oil. Flush cool and keep action light. Skip this step on structured or water-unsafe linen.

spottingProtocol.technique: Low-temp wetclean or dryclean cycle; block flat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Process cool according to label and construction. Air-dry/block flat and inspect fully dry before any heat. No tumble heat or hot press until oil shadow is gone.

steps: 1. Triage care label, construction, dye stability, trim, lining, and age.
2. Work dry-side first with oil spotter / volatile dry solvent on a board, outside-in, blotting to clean absorbent.
3. Pull residual oil/solvent with fuller’s earth or clean absorbent powder; repeat only as needed.
4. If water-safe, cleanup with NSD + cool flush; skip wet step for structured/water-unsafe linen.
5. Cool wetclean/dryclean as appropriate; block/air-dry flat; inspect dry before heat.
products: Dry-side oil spotter / POG as shop-approved
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool / no heat until fully clear
warnings: Do not start with Dawn/toothbrush on a pro linen piece
Do not wet-treat structured, lined, trimmed, vintage, or unstable-dye linen
No hot water and no tumble heat; heat can shrink linen and set oil shadow
Control solvent spread with absorbent underlay and outside-in feathering

homeSolutions.agent: Only Simple Washable Linen Stays Home
homeSolutions.instruction: If the item is structured, lined, trimmed, vintage, dark-dyed, or dry-clean-only, stop and take it to a professional. Simple washable napkins/shirts can use the home method.
Cover fresh oil with cornstarch or baking soda. Let it sit 20–30 minutes, then brush off gently. Do this before adding water.
For simple washable linen only, use a small amount of dish soap with cool water. Work lightly from the outside edge inward, then cool rinse.
Cool wash per care label. Air-dry flat and inspect when dry. Do not use dryer heat until the oily shadow is gone.

steps: 2. Absorb fresh oil with cornstarch/baking soda before water.
3. Light dish-soap treatment with cool water only on simple washable linen.
4. Cool wash, air-dry flat, inspect dry.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the item is structured, lined, trimmed, vintage, dry-clean-only, dye-unstable, already heat-dried, or still shows an oily halo after one careful attempt.
warnings: Professional cleaner for structured or valuable linen

neverDo: Never start pro linen oil work with a home Dawn/toothbrush routine
Never wet-treat structured, lined, trimmed, vintage, or dye-unstable linen
fiberSensitivities: Linen shrinks aggressively under hot water
Structured linen warps under wet treatment

materialWarnings: Professional protocol requires dry-side oil work first
Cool water only when wet-side is safe
Air/block dry flat; no heat until clear
Open weave + oil = fast penetration and halo risk

escalation.when: Structured / lined / trimmed linen, dry-clean-only label, dye instability, vintage piece, hot water / dryer used previously, visible oily halo after first pass, or high-value item.
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking oil on [simple / structured / dyed / vintage] linen, age [hours / days / weeks], home-treated with powder preload + Dawn + cool wash + air-dry flat. No hot water / no chlorine bleach. [Residual oily feel / structured concerns]."

contextQuestions: Type of oil — olive / canola / vegetable / peanut?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Pro sequence starts dry-side with oil spotter/volatile dry solvent and absorbent control. Home-style Dawn/toothbrush language belongs only in simple washable home lane, not the pro protocol. Structured linen needs professional finishing/blocking.
